food_name: Char Siu Bao (Steamed BBQ Pork Bun)
aliases: வேகவைத்த சார் சியு பவ் (Vēkavaittа Cār Ciyū Pav)

tamil: வேகவைத்த சார் சியு பவ் (Vēkavaittа Cār Ciyū Pav)

region_of_origin: Guangdong Province, China; widely consumed in Singapore
food_subgroup: Steamed yeasted white flour bun with BBQ pork filling; dim sum item

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific term for char siu bao (steamed BBQ pork bun). Closest parent would be a filled steamed bun concept; no exact URI available without fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Steamed bun with meat filling — closest FoodEx2 parent category for filled steamed bread products
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for char siu bao specifically. A0FJC (steamed bread/bun) used as closest defensible parent. Char siu filling is not distinguished at this level.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for char siu bao as a specific food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard steamed char siu bao weighs approximately 65 g and provides around 185 kcal, making it a moderate-energy dim sum item typically consumed in sets of two to three at yum cha.

nutrient_source.title: Char Siu Bao (Steamed) — Health Promotion Board Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for steamed char siu bao. All per-100g values preserved from the verified input record which cites HPB as primary source.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Steamed White Flour Bun — analogous estimate based on refined wheat flour steamed bread entries
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for char siu bao (steamed) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 70 (high) is estimated from the closest analogous food: steamed white flour bun / Chinese steamed bread (mantou-type), which is documented at GI ~68–72 in the University of Sydney GI Database. The char siu filling contributes minimal additional carbohydrate-raising effect. GL per 65g serving calculated as: (70 × 29.3g available carbohydrate per 65g serving) / 100 = 20.5. This estimation is consistent with the HPB Singapore source confidence rating of 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content — 492 mg per 100g; approximately 320 mg per 65g bun
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit intake to one bun per meal, as two buns deliver approximately 640 mg sodium — over one-quarter of the 2,300 mg daily limit — before accounting for other yum cha dishes, dipping sauces, or tea accompaniments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI ~70) and high glycemic load (~20.5 per 65g bun) driven by refined white flour bun base
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should treat each steamed char siu bao as a high-GL carbohydrate serving; consuming two buns generates a combined GL of approximately 41, which constitutes a significant glycemic event and should be accounted for within the meal's total carbohydrate budget.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (2.3g/100g; ~1.5g per bun) and elevated sodium (492 mg/100g); cholesterol 25 mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Steamed char siu bao is preferable to baked or fried dim sum alternatives for cardiovascular patients due to lower total fat, but sodium accumulation across multiple buns at yum cha remains a meaningful concern; limiting to one to two buns and avoiding high-sodium dipping sauces is advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Char Siu Bao (叉燒包), the steamed version, is one of the most iconic Cantonese dim sum items — a soft, pillowy white steamed bun that characteristically splits open at the top into a flower-like 'smile' (笑口, xiào kǒu) during steaming, which is considered a quality indicator by experienced dim sum chefs. The bun dough is made from low-protein wheat flour leavened with both baking powder and yeast, producing an extraordinarily light, fluffy, cloud-like texture that is distinctly different from the denser, glazed baked version. The filling is diced char siu pork — Cantonese BBQ pork roasted with honey, soy sauce, hoisin sauce, oyster sauce, and five-spice — bound in a sweet-savoury sauce. At approximately 185 kcal per 65g bun, the steamed version is significantly lighter than the baked char siu bao (~270 kcal) but carries a higher glycemic index (GI ~70, high) because it lacks the fat that moderates digestion in the baked pastry shell. Nutritionally, each bun provides 7.2g protein, 4.2g fat, and 29.3g carbohydrate. Sodium is 320 mg per bun, accumulating rapidly when multiple buns are consumed at yum cha. The primary clinical concern is the high glycemic load (~20.5 per bun) for patients with Type 2 Diabetes, driven by the refined white flour base. Widely available at Singapore yum cha restaurants, dim sum stalls, and 24-hour coffee shops. Clinically classified as a moderate-concern dim sum item — better than fried options but worse than har gau and siu mai for glycemic management.
common_user_queries: steamed char siu bao vs baked

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (exact match for steamed char siu bao). GI estimated from closest analogous food in University of Sydney GI Database (steamed white flour bun, GI ~68–72) with confidence 0.9. All core macros and secondary nutrients are complete and internally consistent. Energy check: (11.1 × 4) + (6.5 × 9) + (45.1 × 4) = 44.4 + 58.5 + 180.4 = 283.3 kcal, consistent with reported 285 kcal (within rounding tolerance).
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for steamed char siu bao not available in HPB Singapore Food Composition Database or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the HPB source entry for this item. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB source — no hydrogenated fats used in standard steamed bun preparation). GI of 70 is an analogous estimate based on steamed white flour bun entries in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for char siu bao (steamed) was located. Two default portions are noted in the source record (65g and 80g); 65g is used as the primary serving size per the input record designation. The input record contained two entries with is_default: true in portions — only 65g is treated as the canonical default here. FoodEx2 code A0FJC used as closest defensible parent for steamed filled bun; this is a closest-parent match, not an exact code.
